text: The robot enters an inflated cavity through a gel port, with the camera inserted through an additional port. The colon is identified and retracted, providing a view towards the pelvis. The uterus is visible in the center, carefully maneuvered alongside blood vessels.

The surgeon operates the camera and instruments, using scissors in the right hand to open standard attachments of the colon with a medial to lateral approach. The surgeon's left hand carefully positions the colon with minimal force to prevent tissue damage. Bleeding is controlled using bipolar cautery, addressing vessels up to five millimeters in size.

The surgeon performs additional dissection on the vessel by mobilizing the colon away from its lateral and medial attachments. Monopolar cautery is used to separate the vessels, allowing them to be safely taken with bipolar cautery. The equivalent of an inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) is coagulated with bipolar cautery and divided with monopolar cautery in a bloodless manner. Subsequently, the equivalent of the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) is identified and similarly treated with bipolar cautery, followed by division with monopolar cautery. All bleeding is successfully controlled.

The surgeon controls the robot and camera to continue dissecting the colon to its junction with the rectum, identifying the superior rectal artery equivalent in the porcine model. The bedside assistant adjusts the robot's position using a laparoscopic grasper. The dissection progresses to mobilize the colon, equivalent to the white line of Toldt dissection, with the spleen visible, indicating the approach up to the splenic flexure.

The surgeon uses monopolar cautery to take down lateral adhesions of the colon. The robot is manipulated to change the camera view, and the bedside assistant gently pushes the robot in while bringing a stapling device to the surgical field. The surgeon assists in positioning the stapler to divide and seal the colon, and the assistant fires the stapler upon the surgeon's command.
After the stapler is fired, the surgeon inspects the staple line on the colon. A small amount of undivided tissue is identified and divided to ensure complete separation.

The surgeon performs a medial to lateral dissection using monopolar scissors and bipolar cautery, starting with the identification of the ureter and proceeding to take the IMA pedicle. The ureter is kept in view during the dissection, which continues up to the white line of TOLT. The robot is repositioned to access the left gutter, and the dissection progresses towards the splenic flexure. The IMV is identified and taken down using bipolar cautery, with the assistance of one external port.

The surgeon continues retroperitoneal dissection, maintaining a position above the ureters. The pelvic dissection begins, targeting the superior rectal artery. The dissection proceeds to the recto sigmoid junction, where a standard EndoGIA stapler is delivered and fired. The staple site is examined, showing complete transection across the junction, marking the completion of the operation.
